summary: Joel announces the release of FogBUGZ 3.0, which expands from simple bug tracking to a broader development management system. The upgrade emphasizes customer feedback integration, allowing companies to handle customer service email through a shared web interface rather than single-user email clients. It supports Extreme Programming-style prioritized feature lists. Joel highlights that FogBUGZ intentionally omits individual performance metrics, arguing that measuring programmers by bug counts causes them to game the system rather than improve quality.
key_points: FogBUGZ 3.0 adds customer feedback integration via email and web, turning customer suggestions into trackable feature items alongside bugs
The email integration component can replace company-wide email aliases with a shared, trackable web interface for customer service
FogBUGZ intentionally omits individual performance metrics because measuring programmers by bug counts causes gaming behavior that makes the tracking system worthless
The product philosophy is to enforce good software development practices by design, including deliberately excluding features that sound useful but reduce effectiveness
money_quotes: As soon as you start measuring people and compensating people based on things like this, they are going to start optimizing their behavior to optimize these numbers, and not in the way you intended.
Every bug report becomes an argument. Programmers insist on recategorizing bugs as 'features.'
Testers are afraid to enter bugs — why antagonize programmers?
The measurements give you what you 'wanted': the number of bugs in the bug tracking system goes down to zero. Of course, there are just as many bugs in the code.
The bug tracking software, hijacked as an HR crutch, becomes worthless for what it was intended for.
We long ago decided that to make FogBUGZ successful, it needs to reflect and enforce as much as we know about good software development practices.
key_insight: Bug tracking tools that provide individual performance metrics undermine their own purpose because developers and testers will game the numbers rather than honestly track defects.
